meta.method: Direct party scores on a 5-point scale (-1 izquierda, -0.5 centro-izquierda, 0 centro, +0.5 centro-derecha, +1 derecha) for every party/coalition reaching >=1% national share (or the 97% cumulative mass) in any CEDE election file 1958-2022, all three bodies. Each row carries the citation that grounds it; rows were drafted by a multi-source research pass (2026-06-05) and consolidated with documented adjudications. Parties with no citable characterization are deliberately UNSCORED (mostly regional/personalist vehicles) and excluded from the score numerator and denominator; per-municipio coverage (scored votes / party-attributed votes) is published alongside every score. Department-level 'COALICION CAMARA/SENADO. X Y Z' lists with no direct row receive the MEAN of their constituent parties that have citable scores (constituent_rules; members without a citable score are omitted from the mean) - these derived values are continuous, NOT restricted to the 5-point scale, and are a documented derivation, not a researched placement. CEDE aggregation buckets ('OTROS PARTIDOS...', 'PARTIDO DESCONOCIDO...') are unscorable by definition. Frente Nacional caveat: presidencia 1962/1966 were alternation elections (fn_consensus flag); anti-FN candidacies in 1962 are recorded as VOTOS NULOS in the source itself (~683k votes incl. Lopez Michelsen's MRL) and are excluded with the partyless rows. THIS TABLE IS SCHOLARLY INTERPRETATION - review pipeline/party_lr.py before citing the memoria view.
meta.encoding: s = round(100 * sum(votos_p*score_p)/sum(votos_p scored)) per municipio, in [-100,100]; 127 = no scored votes. cov = round(100 * scored votes / party-attributed votes). Blanco/nulos and special-constituency votes excluded as in elections.json.

parties_scored.party: COALICION LISTA DE LA DECENCIA. PARTIDO ALIANZA SOCIAL INDEPENDIENTE - UNION PATRIOTICA Y MOVIMIENTO MAIS
parties_scored.basis: Spanish Wikipedia 'Lista de la Decencia': founded 2017 as 'el primer grupo politico de izquierda' (first leftist group) after UP's return, led by Petro, Aida Avella, to 'reforzar la colectividad de izquierda'. Members UP, ASI, MAIS, Colombia Humana. [Explicit left coalition; UP is the historic left. ASI alone is center-left but bloc is left.] (confidence: high)

1994 'Compromiso Colombia' presidential coalition: candidacy of Antonio Navarro Wolff (M-19 / AD-M19), grouping indigenous and leftist movements, with indigenous leader Jesus Pinacue as VP (es.Wikipedia 'Elecciones presidenciales de Colombia de 1994'). Demobilized-guerrilla democratic left. [Scored from lead candidate Navarro Wolff (former M-19 commander); 3.8% in 1994 first round. Despite the centrist-sounding name, this is the left coalition.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. PARTIDO FRENTE SOCIAL Y POLITICO Y VIA ALTERNA
parties_scored.basis: 2002 presidential coalition of Luis Eduardo 'Lucho' Garzon: Frente Social y Politico (created by the CUT trade-union confederation, bringing together left parties/social movements) + Via Alterna (Gustavo Petro's group). Direct precursor to PDI and then Polo Democratico Alternativo (es.Wikipedia 'Luis Eduardo Garzon'; Nueva Sociedad). Left. [Garzon placed 3rd (~6%); union-based broad left. Both constituents are unambiguously left.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. POLO DEMOCRATICO ALTERNATIVO Y UNION PATRIOTICA - COALICION PETRO PRESIDENTE
parties_scored.basis: This is Petro's 2018 first presidential run (Colombia Humana/Decentes + Polo + UP). Described as 'explicitly a left-wing alliance'; Petro reached runoff with historic 41.8% for the left. UP and Polo are left parties; lead = Gustavo Petro. (confidence: high)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Elecciones presidenciales de Colombia de 1982': Frente Democratico was the left-wing coalition (Gerardo Molina's Firmes + the Union Nacional de Oposicion) that nominated intellectual/ex-rector Gerardo Molina for president (~1.2%). Molina was a self-described democratic socialist (CeDInCI Diccionario Biografico de las Izquierdas Latinoamericanas entry). CEDE code 19800001 'FRENTE DEMOCRATICO'. (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: FRENTE POR LA UNIDAD DEL PUEBLO - FUP
parties_scored.basis: Spanish Wikipedia 'Elecciones presidenciales/legislativas de Colombia de 1978' and Redalyc 'Historia de las izquierdas colombianas 1958-2010': FUP was the 1975-1978 left coalition led by the Maoist MOIR (split from UNO with the MAC), 'where the Maoist forces entering the electoral arena converged'; presidential candidate Jaime Piedrahita Cardona 1978. Revolutionary left. (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Alternativa Democrática, Colombia) and scielo.cl: 'Alternativa Democrática' was a left coalition (Carlos Gaviria) grouping FSP, MOIR, Unidad Democrática, Movimiento Ciudadano and AICO, which merged into Polo Democrático Alternativo. [Conflict on dating: the best-documented Alternativa Democrática is the 2002-05 left coalition; the 1994 micro-list of the same name is less documented. Scored left on the consistent left lineage of the name/coalition.] (confidence: medium)

La Silla Vacía / El Tiempo / Wikipedia: FSP was a left front of union leaders, communists, ex-guerrillas and left intellectuals (Lucho Garzón, Carlos Gaviria, Fals Borda); Garzón its 2002 presidential candidate; became part of Polo Democrático. [Left (union/communist/ex-guerrilla bloc).] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO OBRERO INDEPENDIENTE Y REVOLUCIONARIO - MOIR
parties_scored.basis: Wikipedia ES/EN: Maoist Colombian party founded 1970 (Francisco Mosquera), recognized by the Chinese Communist Party as a sister party; advocated New Democracy revolution toward socialism, agrarian revolution; later integrated into Polo Democrático and Dignidad. [Revolutionary/Maoist left.] (confidence: high)

Congreso Visible / La Silla Vacía / Wikipedia (Gustavo Petro): Vía Alterna was the post-AD M-19 left movement of Gustavo Petro and Antonio Navarro (Bogotá, 1998-2002); Petro topped its 2002 Bogotá list; the bloc fed into the Polo Democrático. [Left (ex-M-19 / Petro-Navarro).] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: PACTO HISTORICO: COALICION DE COLOMBIA HUMANA - UNION PATRIOTICA - POLO DEMOCRATICO ALTERNATIVO - PARTIDO DEL TRABAJO - UNIDAD DEMOCRATICA - OTROS
parties_scored.basis: English Wikipedia 'Historic Pact for Colombia': 'left-wing political and electoral coalition' of communists, socialists, social democrats; Petro's vehicle, won 2022 presidency. Britannica/Jacobin/Congress.gov all describe it as the Colombian left. [Lead candidate Gustavo Petro, first leftist president; constituents UP (left), Polo (left), Colombia Humana (left).] (confidence: high)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Alianza Democratica M-19' / Congreso Visible (Uniandes): party formed 1990 from the demobilized M-19 guerrilla together with other demobilized insurgencies and 'a dozen left-wing groups'; M-19 ideology nationalist/Bolivarian, anti-oligarchic, anti-imperialist. Won ~28% of the 1991 Constituent Assembly as the principal alternative-left force; presidential candidate Antonio Navarro Wolff. [M-19 was explicitly non-Marxist-Leninist (unlike FARC/ELN/EPL), but the AD-M19 vehicle aggregated demobilized guerrillas + a dozen left groups and is uniformly classed as the alternative left -> -1.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Comunista Colombiano): Marxist-Leninist party founded 1930, anti-capitalist/anti-imperialist, ran via Unión Patriótica; merged into Pacto Histórico in 2025. [Communist left.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Alternativa Democrática) / scielo.cl: Unidad Democrática was one of the left movements grouped within Carlos Gaviria's Alternativa Democrática (with FSP, MOIR, Movimiento Ciudadano, AICO), feeding into the Polo Democrático. [Left bloc constituent.] (confidence: medium)

English Wikipedia 'Alternative Democratic Pole' infobox: ideology 'Social democracy, Democratic socialism, Progressivism, Protectionism, Camilism'; political position 'Left-wing'. Founding alliance of left parties (PDI + AD). (confidence: high)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Union Nacional de Oposicion (Colombia)': leftist electoral coalition (1972-1982) formed by the Partido Comunista Colombiano, the Maoist MOIR, and the Marxist (Bayona/Echeverri) faction of ANAPO, to group organized left forces during the National Front. Presidential candidate Hernando Echeverri 1974. (confidence: high)

English/Spanish Wikipedia 'Patriotic Union (Colombia)' and Britannica: leftist party founded 1985 by the FARC and the Partido Comunista Colombiano during the Betancur peace talks; 1980s ideology 'openly communist and Marxist'; platform of agrarian reform, nationalization of natural resources, economic model separate from global capitalism. Subsequently exterminated (5,733+ militants murdered). (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: ALTERNATIVOS - COALICION ALIANZA VERDE Y POLO DEMOCRATICO ALTERNATIVO
parties_scored.basis: 2022 Camara coalition of Alianza Verde (center-left, Wikipedia) and Polo Democratico (left, Wikipedia); part of the 'alternative bloc' aligned with the incoming Petro government per Infobae/Alianza Verde communique. [Constituents: Verde center-left (-0.5), Polo left (-1); coalition midpoint center-left. Scored -0.5.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. PARTIDO LIBERAL Y PARTIDO ALIANZA SOCIAL INDEPENDIENTE
parties_scored.basis: Humberto de la Calle 2018 run for Partido Liberal + ASI. Colombian Liberal Party characterized as center-left (ColombiaOne 2026: 'center-left, individual rights, social progress, mixed economy'); ASI is center-left (Wikipedia). Candidate de la Calle = chief FARC-peace negotiator, pro-peace-accord. [Both constituents center-left; lead candidate de la Calle peace-accords defender.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. PARTIDO POLO DEMOCRATICO - PARTIDO ALIANZA VERDE Y MOVIMIENTO COMPROMISO CIUDADANO - COALICION COLOMBIA
parties_scored.basis: El Tiempo/Semana/El Colombiano: 'Coalicion Colombia' 2018 = Fajardo (Compromiso Ciudadano) + Claudia Lopez (Alianza Verde) + Robledo (Polo), described as 'center-left political positioning', anti-corruption + defense of peace accords. Lead candidate Sergio Fajardo, centrist/progressive center. [Constituents: Polo (left), Verde (center-left), Compromiso (center). Coalition characterized center-left; Fajardo himself centrist.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: FUERZA CIUDADANA LA FUERZA DEL CAMBIO
parties_scored.basis: Spanish Wikipedia 'Fuerza Ciudadana (Colombia)' + La Silla Vacia ('la izquierda de Caicedo en Magdalena'): centrist-left progressive/ecological movement of Carlos Caicedo aiming to build a 'federal socialdemocratic state'; peace, gender equity, anti-racism. [La Silla Vacia frames Caicedo as the regional left; party self-describes social-democratic / centrist-left. Could arguably be -1; -0.5 chosen as social-democratic.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Alianza Social Independiente) and El Tiempo: party 'considered to be center-left', born from indigenous struggle (inspired by Manuel Quintín Lame), multiethnic; provided the vehicle for progressive figures like Mockus and Fajardo. [Explicitly center-left in Wikipedia ES.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Apertura Liberal): liberal-oriented movement, 'independiente del liberalismo oficial', founded 1993 by Miguel Ángel Flórez in Norte de Santander; Liberal lineage placed center-left by Congreso Visible. [Personalist/clientelist regional vehicle (later DMG-linked); placement rests on the self-declared liberal lineage rather than a programmatic source.] (confidence: low)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO AUTORIDADES INDIGENAS DE COLOMBIA - AICO
parties_scored.basis: Wikipedia EN (Indigenous Authorities of Colombia) and party site: described as a 'progressive indigenist political party'; principles of territoriality, autonomy, interculturality, defense of ethnic minorities; a constituent of the left Alternativa Democrática. [Progressive indigenist → center-left.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Alternativa Democrática) / scielo.cl: Movimiento Ciudadano is listed as one of the left movements grouped within Carlos Gaviria's Alternativa Democrática coalition (alongside FSP, MOIR, Unidad Democrática, AICO). [Documented as a constituent of the left Alternativa Democrática bloc.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO LIBERALISMO INDEPENDIENTE DE RESTITUCION - LIDER
parties_scored.basis: Name self-identifies as a 'liberalismo independiente' (independent liberal) vehicle; the Colombian Liberal Party is placed by Congreso Visible as 'centro que tiende a la izquierda' and is a Socialist International member. [No specific historiographic source on this 1991-94 micro-vehicle found; placement rests only on the self-declared liberal lineage in the name. Treat as weak.] (confidence: low)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Nuevo Liberalismo' infobox: historical political position (1979-1989, the Galan era covering this dataset's electoral mass) = 'Centroizquierda' (centre-left); ideology liberalismo social/cultural/clasico. Founded 1979 by Luis Carlos Galan as a reformist, anti-clientelist, anti-narco dissidence of the Liberal Party. [Conflict: the same infobox lists the 2021 refounding as 'Centro'. Dataset row spans 1982-2022, but the substantive votes are the 1982-1989 Galan period -> -0.5 (centre-left) is the better-evidenced placement for the relevant window.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Verde Oxígeno): Ingrid Betancourt's movement, originally Oxígeno Liberal (1998), 'ecological, democratic and anti-corruption', renamed Partido Verde Oxígeno in 2000. [Green/anti-corruption progressive-liberal; dissident-liberal lineage.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: ORGANIZACION NACIONAL INDIGENA DE COLOMBIA - ONIC
parties_scored.basis: Dejusticia / redalyc / ONIC: the national indigenous organization; ran for the Senate special indigenous district in 1991; agenda of indigenous rights, self-determination, pluriethnic recognition — progressive indigenist, same family as ASI/AICO. [Progressive indigenist → center-left.] (confidence: medium)

English Wikipedia 'Green Alliance (Colombia)' infobox: ideology 'Green politics, Progressivism'; political position 'Centre-left'. ColombiaOne 2026 also places it 'centre-left progressive'. (confidence: high)

English Wikipedia 'Colombian Liberal Party' infobox lists political position as 'Centre to centre-left' and ideologies Social liberalism + Social democracy; 'founded as a classical liberal party but later developed a more social-democratic tradition, joining the Socialist International in 1999.' Consistent with standard expert-survey placement of the PLC left-of-center. [Straddles centre / centre-left; over 1958-2022 it sat right-of the new left but left-of the Conservatives. -0.5 captures the social-democratic post-1990s identity; a 0 could be argued for the mid-century clientelist machine, but the social-liberal characterization dominates.] (confidence: high)

Huber & Stephens 'Latin America and Caribbean Political Dataset 1945-2012' codebook classifies the Partido Social Demócrata of Colombia under 'Secular C. Left' (secular center-left); social-democratic family. PSDC was a constituent of the left Frente Social y Político. [Social democracy → center-left.] (confidence: medium)

Party name self-identifies as 'Socialista Democrático' (democratic socialist); the historical Partido Socialista Democrático of Colombia (1939-49) was the renamed Communist Party. The 2002 micro-vehicle's only documented signal is its socialist-democratic self-label. [No historiographic source on the specific 2002 vehicle; placement rests only on the self-declared democratic-socialist name. Weak.] (confidence: low)

English Wikipedia 'Partido Verde Oxigeno' infobox lists FORMER position (Betancourt era, relevant to 2006) as 'Center to center-left', former ideology 'Environmentalism, Pacifism'. Founded 1998 by Ingrid Betancourt as anticorruption/ecological liberal party. [Party shifted to 'center-right to right-wing / green conservatism' only in later (post-2021) revival. The 2006 dataset entry corresponds to the historical center/center-left Betancourt party.] (confidence: medium)

Cesar Ayala Diago, 'La explosion del populismo en Colombia: Anapo' (Univ. Nacional, 2011) frames ANAPO as the defining populist movement of the National Front era. Wikipedia/Encyclopedia.com: nationalist + populist catch-all uniting dissidents from BOTH traditional parties plus moderate socialists; self-defined as 'nationalist, revolutionary and popular' pursuing a Colombian-style 'socialism', yet led by ex-military ruler Rojas Pinilla. A documented movement that genuinely resists the left-right axis. [Conflict: some sources call it 'left-wing nationalist' (its later self-description and the M-19 lineage of dissident anapistas pull toward -0.5/-1), while its conservative absorptions and personalist Rojas leadership pull right. Per the brief's allowance for documented-but-unplaceable populism, scored 0; a defensible alternative is -0.5 if weighting the late left-populist drift.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: CENTRO ESPERANZA: COALICION DE PARTIDO DIGNIDAD - NUEVO LIBERALISMO - COLOMBIA RENACIENTE - ALIANZA SOCIAL INDEPENDIENTE - OTROS
parties_scored.basis: es.Wikipedia 'Coalicion Centro Esperanza': 2022 coalition of center and center-left parties/movements (Compromiso Ciudadano/Fajardo, Dignidad/Robledo, Nuevo Liberalismo/Galan, En Marcha, ASI); self-defined as 'la cara visible del centro' (Pares). Fajardo won the consulta. [Explicitly the political-center coalition; constituents lean center to center-left. Scored 0 (center) as the documented self-identity; a -0.5 center-left reading is defensible given ASI/Dignidad/Nuevo Liberalismo. | ADJUDICATED: explicit self-identity and dominant press framing is 'el centro'; the center-left reading (constituents Dignidad/NL/ASI) noted.] (confidence: high)

English Wikipedia 'Sergio Fajardo' and Compromiso Ciudadano coverage: Fajardo's movement; he 'brands himself as a pragmatic politician with no particular ideology', labeled centrist; 'Colombian progressive center', critical of both neoliberalism and leftist statism. [Center / progressive-center; could lean -0.5. Scored 0 as documented centrist.] (confidence: medium)

Searches (Kellogg working paper on party atomization; press) describe it as a parapolitics vehicle 'constituted practically only of parapolíticos' with candidate-endorsement sold as a business; no documented programmatic left-right ideology. [Personalist/regional parapolitics vehicle without a coherent ideology; included at neutral only because it is documented, but effectively unplaceable — see unscorable rationale.] (confidence: low)

No citable left-right characterization found despite targeted searching; generic 'popular participation' civic label. [Effectively unplaceable; included at neutral as documented-but-unplaceable, but see unscorable reasoning — no source establishes ideology.] (confidence: low)

1998 'Opcion Vida' was Noemi Sanin's third-force ('terceria') coalition (with Antanas Mockus, Carlos Lleras de la Fuente), winning 26.9% as an independent outside the bipartisan system (es.Wikipedia 'Elecciones presidenciales de Colombia de 1998'). Centrist anti-establishment third force. [Consistent with Sanin's later centrist 'Si Colombia' (2002). Center placement reflects its documented independent third-force identity rather than her Conservative origins.] (confidence: medium)

No citable ideological characterization found despite searching; name is a generic 'renovation' label with no documented programmatic content. [Borderline unscorable; left at neutral 0 only as a placeholder — better treated as unscorable (see note). No source establishes a left-right position.] (confidence: low)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO POLITICO CIEN POR CIENTO POR COLOMBIA
parties_scored.basis: Spanish Wikipedia 'Movimiento Politico Cien por Ciento por Colombia': an ethnic-minority party that retained legal status via minority-protection norms (one seat). No left-right ideological characterization found. [Ethnic-minority single-constituency vehicle; no axis placement in sources. Scored 0 as documented-but-unplaceable; defensible as unscorable.] (confidence: low)

Wikipedia ES (Elecciones presidenciales 2002) / Semana: Noemí Sanín's independent movement, self-positioned as centrist ('center-oriented'); Sanín left the Conservative Party in 1998 to run as an independent. [Self-identified center; ex-Conservative leader running as independent centrist.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Movimiento Unitario Metapolítico; Regina Betancourt de Liska / 'Regina 11'): self-defined 'metaphysical, philosophical, socioeconomic and political' movement, esoteric/personalist, explicitly 'metapolitical' ('goes beyond politics'), motto 'health, money, love and happiness'. [Documented but genuinely unplaceable on the left-right axis — esoteric personalist movement. | ADJUDICATED: both researchers call the axis placement shaky for this esoteric-personalist vehicle; the single 'centroderecha' infobox reading is recorded here but 0 (documented, axis-resistant) is retained.] (confidence: medium)

Party website/El Tiempo/Pares: Afro-descendant ethnic party (special Afro circunscription), Ubuntu philosophy 'We Are One With All', self-described 'broad center-front movement... based on social policies and respects private property'. Founded by ex-Santos ministers. [Self-describes 'centro' front; ethnic-representation vehicle, weak axis placement. Scored 0 (center) per self-description; borderline unscorable.] (confidence: low)

English Wikipedia 'Social Action Party (Colombia)': minor 2006 party, one Chamber seat (0.60%); leaders later 'moved into an NGO advocating preservation of water resources'. No left-right ideological characterization stated in any source found. [Environmental/water-focused leaders post-politics suggest non-axis single-issue; no citable left-right placement, scored 0 (genuinely unplaceable-but-documented). Could be argued unscorable.] (confidence: low)

Mockus' movement (Visionarios). Mockus is the canonical Colombian centrist; Wikipedia/Sergio Fajardo associations and Mockus' own deliberative-democracy/pedagogy program place him at the center, not on the economic left-right axis. Later folded into Alianza Verde (center-left). [Mockus widely characterized as centrist/independent; party platform is civic-pedagogical, not class/economic.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. PARTIDO CAMBIO RADICAL Y PARTIDO DE LA U - #MEJOR VARGAS LLERAS
parties_scored.basis: German Vargas Lleras 2018 presidential run (#MejorVargasLleras), backed by Cambio Radical and Partido de la U; El Colombiano/Semana describe him as a centre-right candidate with backing from conservative sectors. [Constituents both centre-right (CR +0.5, La U +0.5); candidate Vargas Lleras centre-right.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: COALICION PRESIDENCIA. PARTIDO DE LA U - CAMBIO RADICAL Y PARTIDO LIBERAL. UNIDAD NACIONAL
parties_scored.basis: Coalition = Santos 2014 reelection 'Unidad Nacional' (La U + Cambio Radical + Liberal). Spanish Wikipedia 'Unidad Nacional (Colombia)' describes it as a coalition of 'center-right official political parties'. Lead = Juan Manuel Santos. [Scored from constituents: La U (+0.5), Cambio Radical (+0.5); Liberal is center-left but coalition's center of gravity and Santos government label is centre-right.] (confidence: high)

CEDE code 19829998 appears ONLY in the 1982 presidencia file (verified against 1982 camara/senado: 0 rows) and corresponds to Belisario Betancur's Movimiento Nacional coalition candidacy (46.8%, winner) - the conservative-led, Betancur-headed coalition scored +0.5 like its 1978 'MOVIMIENTO NACIONAL' predecessor. [Same code is a generic aggregation bucket in other cycles; safe here because it occurs in no other 1982 file.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Elecciones presidenciales 1998) and PDBA Georgetown: the 1998 winning coalition was Andrés Pastrana's 'Gran Alianza por el Cambio' — Conservative Party plus liberal dissidents and independents, led by the Conservative/center-right Pastrana. [Aggregate coalition label (51.9% share); scored from the documented ideology of lead candidate Pastrana (Conservative/center-right). It was a broad big-tent, so this is the lead-candidate placement only.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: EQUIPO POR COLOMBIA: COALICION PARTIDO CONSERVADOR COLOMBIANO - PARTIDO MIRA - CREEMOS COLOMBIA - PARTIDO DE LA UNION POR LA GENTE - VALOS PALANTE CON ECHEVERRY - OTROS
parties_scored.basis: 2022 'Equipo por Colombia' described by El Tiempo/El Espectador as a center-right alliance representing a sector of the right; won by Federico 'Fico' Gutierrez (Creemos). Constituents: Conservative Party (right), Creemos, MIRA (Christian center-right), Partido de la U (center-right); later backed by the Liberal Party. [CNN labeled Fico 'la derecha'; coalition framing is center-right. Mixed center-right-to-right composition; scored +0.5 as the dominant 'center-right alliance' characterization, with the Conservative core and Fico's security-right discourse pushing toward +1. | ADJUDICATED: modal Colombian press framing is 'centroderecha' and the constituent mix is center-right-to-right; en.wiki's 'centre-right and right-wing' reading noted.] (confidence: high)

Spanish-language sources (Las2Orillas / Wikipedia ALAS) describe Alas Equipo Colombia as a 'uribista' centre-right party (fusion of ALAS and Equipo Colombia), defender of Uribe's security policy; later sank in parapolitica scandal. [Center-right uribista; head of caucus Alvaro Araujo convicted for paramilitary ties.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO ALTERNATIVO DE AVANZADA SOCIAL - ALAS
parties_scored.basis: Wikipedia ES (Alternativa Liberal de Avanzada Social) and La Silla Vacía: ALAS, founded by Álvaro Araújo Castro as a Liberal dissidence, merged into 'Alas Equipo Colombia', defined as uribista, center-right. [Center-right uribista (later parapolitics-tainted).] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Cambio Radical): considered center-right; founded by ex-galanista Liberal dissidents (1997-2000); economic liberalism + security hardline; supported Uribe in 2002. [Center-right.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Convergencia Ciudadana) and Semana: founded by Luis Alberto Gil (ex-M19); 'en principio era de izquierda' but joined Álvaro Uribe's government coalition and was a core parapolitics party (became PIN/Opción Ciudadana). [Conflict: leftist origins vs. uribista governing alignment. Net documented trajectory is a clientelist machine integrated into the (right) uribista coalition; scored center-right on its operative alignment.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO INDEPENDIENTE DE RENOVACION ABSOLUTA - MIRA
parties_scored.basis: Wikipedia ES (Partido Político MIRA) classifies its ideology as 'democracia cristiana' (Christian democracy); EN Wikipedia and academic literature describe it as a confessional party of the IDMJI church opposed to abortion, euthanasia and same-sex marriage (social conservatism), while it self-describes as transcending left/right ('miraísmo'). Christian-democratic + social-conservative maps to center-right. [MIRA explicitly rejects left-right placement and combines pro-poor/welfare economics with moral conservatism; placed +0.5 on the documented Christian-democratic/social-conservative reading, but it is genuinely cross-cutting.] (confidence: medium)

CEDE party-code table (data/raw/cede/electoral/Partidos_Electorales.dta) keys this string to code 19780001 (a 1978 vehicle, distinct from 1992's 'Movimiento Nacional Conservador' code 19920005). Spanish Wikipedia 'Elecciones presidenciales de Colombia de 1978' (confirmed via WebFetch): 'Movimiento Nacional' was the coalition behind Conservative Belisario Betancur, composed of the Conservative Party, the ANAPO majority, dissident anti-Turbay liberals, and the Christian Democracy party. Betancur was a Conservative of the party's progressive wing. [Conservative-LED but heterogeneous (folded in ANAPO + Christian Democrats) and headed by Betancur, 'el conservador mas liberal' (El Espectador), known for the most progressive thinking of any 20th-c. president. Lead-candidate rule -> right core, but the broad coalition + reformist Betancur pull it to centre-right (+0.5) rather than hard right (+1). Peak 46.6% share matches Betancur's strong 1978 run.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Nueva Fuerza Democrática) and CIDOB: center-right party founded by Andrés Pastrana as a dissidence from the Conservative Party; defense of private property/private initiative, anti-narcotics, military modernization; Pastrana governed 1998-2002. [Explicitly 'centroderecha' in Wikipedia ES.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO RENOVADOR DE ACCION SOCIAL - MORAL
parties_scored.basis: Congreso Visible / PDBA Georgetown: MORAL's 2002 Senate seat was won by Mario Uribe Escobar (cousin and close ally of Álvaro Uribe, founder of the uribista Colombia Democrática); the movement later merged into Cambio Radical. [Mario Uribe's uribista vehicle → center-right; later convicted in parapolitics.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Nueva Fuerza Democrática): NFD is Andrés Pastrana's center-right Conservative dissidence; the 1994 'Equipo Unionista' Senate label was an NFD/Conservative-unionist list within that lineage. [NFD-lineage center-right (Conservative unionism).] (confidence: medium)

English Wikipedia 'Cambio Radical' infobox: ideology 'Conservative liberalism'; political position 'Centre to centre-right'. ColombiaReports and Bogota Post describe it as pro-business, pro-growth centre-right under Vargas Lleras. [Vargas Lleras is characterized as pragmatic rather than ideological but consistently placed centre-right.] (confidence: high)

parties_scored.party: PARTIDO SOCIAL DE UNIDAD NACIONAL - PARTIDO DE LA U
parties_scored.basis: English Wikipedia 'Partido de la U' infobox: ideology 'Social liberalism, Third Way, Liberalism (Colombian), Catch-all party' (historically Uribism); political position 'Centre-right' (footnote notes centre-left/centre-right depending on source). Founded by Uribe allies, later Santos' governing party. [Self-describes as third-way/liberal; some sources (e.g. ColombiaOne 2026) call it 'center'. Centre-right is the better-evidenced placement per Wikipedia and its Uribista security origins.] (confidence: high)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Partido Somos Region Colombia': party that recovered the legal status of the defunct 'Alas Equipo Colombia', described as a 'center-right Colombian political party'; focused on regional decentralization. [Continuation of centre-right uribista-lineage Alas Equipo Colombia. 2018 presidential candidate Viviane Morales (conservative).] (confidence: medium)

es.Wikipedia 'Primero Colombia': citizen movement that carried Alvaro Uribe to the presidency in 2002 and reelection in 2006 on the 'Democratic Security' platform. Uribismo = security-hardline neoliberal right (see CJLACS 2019 'The Colombian Right'). [Scored from lead candidate Uribe, whose ideology is the canonical Colombian right.] (confidence: high)

Andres Pastrana's 1994 presidential coalition: nominated by the Colombian Conservative Party, uniting Nueva Fuerza Democratica + the Conservative Party (en.Wikipedia 'Andres Pastrana Arango'). Lead candidate and party are traditional conservative right. [Scored from lead candidate/party (Pastrana, Conservative). Lost 1994 runoff to Samper by ~2%.] (confidence: high)

English Wikipedia 'League of Anti-Corruption Governors' infobox: ideology 'Populism, Liberalism, Neoliberalism, Catch-all'; political position 'Far-right' (some sources centre-right/right). Rodolfo Hernandez characterized by NYT as right-wing, others far-right; right-populist 'state like a private business' rhetoric. [Hernandez claimed no left/right; Reuters said centre-right, NYT right-wing, others far-right. Chose right (+1) per dominant right-populist characterization; conflict noted.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia EN (Colombia Always) / Congreso Visible: described as a national-conservative party; senators incl. Germán Vargas Lleras and Nancy Patricia Gutiérrez; supported Uribe in 2002. [National-conservative, pro-Uribe; Vargas Lleras' pre-Cambio-Radical vehicle.] (confidence: medium)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Movimiento Colombia Viva' infobox: ideology 'Uribismo, Liberalism, Populism'; political position 'Derecha' (Right). Joined uribista coalition 2006; leaders (Dieb Maloof et al.) convicted/jailed in parapolitica. [Right-wing uribista populist.] (confidence: medium)

Search of Congreso Visible / secondary sources: C4 (Jimmy Chamorro, Cruzada Estudiantil y Profesional) ideological platform 'founded on principles and teachings from the Bible', 'conservative in character, promoting Christian values', part of a pro-life Christian coalition. [Evangelical social-conservative party; same confessional-right cluster as UC and PNC.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Conservador) and El Tiempo/Congreso Visible: explicit Conservative Party dissidence ('Conservatismo Independiente de la familia Gerlein', Atlántico; leader Roberto Gerlein, 'Colombia es un país conservador'). [Traditional conservatism (Gerlein machine in Barranquilla).] (confidence: high)

Spanish/English Wikipedia 'Movimiento de Salvacion Nacional' / 'National Salvation Movement': conservative party created 1990 by Alvaro Gomez Hurtado (son of hardline conservative pres. Laureano Gomez) as a conservative dissidence, 'conservative ideology and right-wing position', targeting urban right-wing opinion. El Espectador 2022 quotes its list-head: 'Aca no hay extrema derecha, solo derecha.' (confidence: high)

El Tiempo / El Colombiano: Equipo Colombia was a Conservative-origin movement led by Luis Alfredo Ramos in Antioquia; pro-Uribe (Ramos as Senate president inaugurated Uribe in 2002); later fused into Alas Equipo Colombia (center-right uribista). [Conservative/uribista right.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia EN (Harold Bedoya Pizarro): founded 1997-98 by Gen. Harold Bedoya (ex-armed-forces commander); hardline security candidacy — anti-FARC, refusal to negotiate, anti-drug priority. [Security-hardline right.] (confidence: high)

Wikipedia ES (Juan Gómez Martínez) and La Silla Vacía (Fabio Valencia Cossio): Fuerza Progresista was Juan Gómez Martínez's regional movement, an 'independent movement within conservatism' in Antioquia, part of/aligned with the Conservative Party. [Conservative dissidence; 'progresista' in the name refers to its leader's faction, not left progressivism.] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia ES (Partido Conservador) and scielo.org.mx (Duque, 'Partidos divididos'): created 1986 by congressman Gustavo Rodríguez as one of the internal/dissident movements of the Colombian Conservative Party. [Conservative dissidence.] (confidence: medium)

Fundación Carolina / press: Catholic lay confessional movement led by ex-senator Carlos Corsi Otálora (a traditionalist Catholic), pro-life religious electoral vehicle; later merged into Cambio Radical. [Confessional Catholic traditionalist movement → moral/social right.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: MOVIMIENTO UNICO DE RENOVACION CONSERVADORA - MURCO
parties_scored.basis: El Tiempo archive: MURCO was a conservative renovation movement (associated with Jorge Sedano González, Santander); name self-identifies as conservative ('Renovación Conservadora'). [Thin sourcing; placement on the explicit conservative self-label plus Conservative-dissidence context.] (confidence: low)

El Tiempo and Razón Pública: evangelical confessional movement (allied with Consejo Evangélico and Partido Nacional Cristiano in the 1991 Constituent Assembly); Pentecostal parties characterized as militantly opposed to abortion decriminalization and same-sex couples, defending 'morality and the traditional family' — i.e. the Christian/social-conservative right. [Pares chapter on protestant/evangelical parties characterizes this confessional cluster as right/conservative on moral issues.] (confidence: medium)

parties_scored.party: PARTIDO CENTRO DEMOCRATICO - MANO FIRME CORAZON GRANDE
parties_scored.basis: English Wikipedia 'Democratic Centre (Colombia)' infobox: ideology 'Conservatism, Economic liberalism, Right-wing populism'; political position 'Right-wing to far-right'. Uribe's party; ColombiaOne 2026 'ideological right and far-right', hard security + free-market. (confidence: high)

Spanish Wikipedia 'Partido Colombia Democratica' and Uribismo entry: uribista party led by Mario Uribe (Uribe's cousin); ideology situated in 'nacionalismo conservador y la derecha' (nationalist conservatism and the right). Decimated by parapolitica scandal 2006. [Core uribista right vehicle.] (confidence: medium)

Spanish sources (Pares, El Universal, Congreso Visible): evangelical Christian party founded 2017, 'conservadurismo cristiano', 'principios democrata cristianos de derecha', characterized as 'derecha radical', anti-abortion, anti same-sex marriage. [Christian-conservative right.] (confidence: high)

English Wikipedia 'Colombian Conservative Party': 'a conservative political party'; historically supported close cooperation with the Roman Catholic Church, intolerance of non-Catholic beliefs, maintenance of class privileges, and highly centralized government. Uncontested historiography places it as the traditional Colombian right. (confidence: high)

ColombiaReports: PIN (National Integration Party, 2009 rename of Convergencia Ciudadana, later Opcion Ciudadana) is a right-wing party widely linked to the parapolitica scandal; many of its 2010 candidates had familial ties to imprisoned parapolitics-convicted politicians. [Same lineage scored as Opcion Ciudadana (+1).] (confidence: medium)

Wikipedia EN/las2orillas: founded 1989 by pastors César and Claudia Castellanos (Misión Carismática Internacional); evangelical confessional party whose political capital was the religious authority of its pastor-founders; same moral-conservative evangelical cluster as UC/C4. [Pentecostal confessional party; characterized as right/conservative on moral issues.] (confidence: medium)

ColombiaReports: 'Citizens' Choice (Opcion Ciudadana) is a right wing party' that renamed from PIN/Convergencia Ciudadana in 2013 to distance from the parapolitica scandal. Heavily linked to paramilitary-tied politicians. [Same lineage as PIN (next entry); right-wing per ColombiaReports profile.] (confidence: medium)

constituent_rules.pattern: PARTIDO DE LA U\b|DE LA U -

meta.fn_note: 1958-1974 Frente Nacional: presidencia 1962/1966 were alternation elections where one traditional party did not compete (rec.fn_consensus=true); congressional races remained contested between the two parties.
